title: TPU v1 · Signoff Static Timing Analysis: Exhaustive Setup and Hold at 700 MHz with Extracted SPEF
oneLine: Signoff STA gives the final verdict on whether 700 MHz is real. Using layout-extracted SPEF, it statically analyzes every PE-to-PE, control, and UB→array→accumulator path across worst PVT corners. Setup asks whether data arrives early enough; hold asks whether it changes too soon. Only nonnegative setup and hold slack on every path in every required mode/corner supports 700 MHz as a final specification.

legend.name: Launch FF / capture FF
legend.desc: STA decomposes the design into launch-register → combinational-logic → capture-register paths. The launch FF emits data at a 700 MHz edge; the data must stabilize before the next capture edge. This path is the fundamental unit for judging timing closure.

legend.name: Combinational path + extracted SPEF
legend.desc: The path may be one PE stage, control decode, or UB-to-array datapath. Signoff delay combines cell timing with R and C extracted from actual routed geometry, replacing pre-layout estimates with values representative of the silicon implementation.

legend.name: Setup check (fast enough; frequency-dependent)
legend.desc: Setup requires data to stabilize before the capture edge by at least the setup time. setup slack = required − arrival should be ≥0. A setup miss may be relieved by a longer clock period, but then the 700 MHz performance claim decreases.

legend.name: Hold check (not too fast; mandatory fix)
legend.desc: Hold requires old data to remain stable after the capture edge for the hold time. A too-fast new value can overwrite it. Hold is essentially independent of clock period, so lowering frequency does not fix it; insert delay and clear every violation.

legend.name: Exhaustive vectorless coverage
legend.desc: STA uses the timing graph to enumerate paths without simulation vectors. A 256×256 array contains too many paths for dynamic timing simulation to cover exhaustively, while STA reports worst-case setup and hold across all of them.

steps.cap: ① Decompose the design into launch FF → combinational logic → capture FF paths, including PE links, control logic, and global UB→array→accumulator paths.
② Use layout-extracted SPEF for real wire/cell delay and the implemented clock tree for launch/capture arrival and skew.
③ Enumerate all paths statically without stimulus; vectorless coverage supplies the exhaustive basis that simulation cannot provide.
④ Check setup: data must stabilize before the capture edge by at least setup time; illustrative criterion slack ≥0. Lower frequency can help but reduces the 700 MHz claim.
⑤ Check hold: data must remain stable after the edge by at least hold time. Frequency cannot repair hold; every violation must be eliminated.

before: Prepare extracted SPEF: Run post-route RC extraction for every net. Signoff timing must use real R, C, and coupling from the implemented layout rather than early estimates.

Define the MMMC corner set: Combine process, voltage, temperature, and RC corners with operating modes. Slow/low-voltage/hot conditions often dominate setup; fast/high-voltage/cold often dominate hold.
Define modes and SDC: Provide functional and test/scan modes, the 700 MHz clock, I/O timing, and justified multicycle/false-path exceptions. Bad exceptions create false failures or hide real ones.

Set criteria and the signoff engine: Require setup slack ≥0 and hold slack ≥0 on every real path in every required mode/corner, with zero hold violations. PrimeTime-class STA is the standard golden analysis.

Set OCV/derating and waiver policy: Apply OCV/AOCV/POCV margins for manufacturing variation and require evidence and approval for every legitimate exception; never hide a real path casually with set_false_path.
during: Core rule: STA is vectorless and exhaustive. It traverses the timing graph and solves worst delay for every launch-to-capture path without stimulus. That coverage is essential for a path-rich 256×256 array.

Check setup and hold together but treat them differently. Setup asks whether slow paths complete in time, typically at slow corners; hold asks whether fast paths change too soon, typically at fast corners. Both require their own worst-case corners.
Setup may be rescued by frequency, at a performance cost. Extending the clock period restores setup margin, but TPU's ~92 TOPS depends on 700 MHz, so frequency reduction is a last resort.

Hold is a hard defect and must be fully cleared. Because hold does not improve with a longer period, add delay buffers to every fast failing path. One remaining hold violation can break function at any frequency.

Use real path and clock data. Path delay comes from SPEF, clock arrival and skew from the implemented clock tree, and variation from OCV/derating. Signoff is valid only when all inputs represent the final implementation.

State the boundary honestly: SPEF-based STA, MMMC, OCV, PrimeTime, and setup/hold criteria are standard signoff practice. TPU's actual SPEF, corners, reports, and slack are unpublished; all thresholds here are illustrative.

result.label: Signoff scope
result.value: All PE links + control paths + global UB→array→accumulator paths (vectorless exhaustive analysis)

result.label: Delay / clock inputs
result.value: Layout-extracted SPEF + implemented clock tree with skew + OCV/derating

result.label: Setup criterion (example)
result.value: All paths · slow corners · setup slack ≥0; frequency reduction is possible but sacrifices 700 MHz

result.label: Hold criterion (example)
result.value: All paths · fast corners · hold slack ≥0; mandatory zero-violation closure

result.label: Coverage matrix
result.value: All required PVT/RC corners × all operating modes under MMMC

result.label: Primary conclusion
result.value: Setup/hold pass at 700 MHz → frequency can enter final specification; PrimeTime report is golden evidence

after: Repair setup failures in ⑧: Upsize cells, buffer or restructure paths, adjust useful skew, or as a last resort lower frequency and revise the signoff maximum.
Repair every hold failure with delay: Insert delay buffers on each fast path and rerun analysis. Hold is zero tolerance and cannot proceed with any violation.
Re-extract after ECO and re-sign: Any layout ECO changes parasitics. Extract updated SPEF and rerun STA, ensuring hold fixes did not create setup failures or vice versa.
Feed STA into the tapeout gate: Combine all-mode/all-corner timing results with EM/IR, DRC/LVS, LEC, and other signoff dimensions. Only a green timing cell permits 700 MHz in the final specification.
Release only after STA is fully green: Exhaustive setup and hold closure across the array and global paths provides the final timing evidence for 700 MHz/~92 TOPS and tapeout.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (SPEF extraction, MMMC, OCV/derating, PrimeTime-class STA, and setup/hold criteria are standard signoff practice. TPU-specific SPEF, corners, timing reports, and slack are not public; all thresholds and frequency-reduction discussion are illustrative.)
